pi_items.verified_value: Real file exists at source location: benchmarks/cua_world/environments/equest_env/data/4StoreyBuilding.inp (94,825 bytes, 3,258 lines)
pi_items.source: File system verification and environment documentation (evidence_docs/README.md)

Industry standards: 25kW is a practical size for commercial building rooftop PV (confirmed via energy-models.com PV training documentation and multiple solar design guides). Real-world 25kW systems are commercially available and widely implemented.

Solar panel design standards (unboundsolar.com, solarpaneltilt.com, cleanenergyreviews.info): 20-degree tilt is widely used in utility-scale installations (>50% of Minnesota installations use 20-30 degree range). Results in <5% annual energy reduction vs optimal latitude-based angle.

Solar panel orientation standards (sunrun.com, unboundsolar.com): 180 degrees is true south, the optimal azimuth for Northern Hemisphere installations. South-facing orientation improves efficiency by up to 30% vs non-optimal directions.

Photovoltaic technology standards (cleanenergyreviews.info, Fraunhofer ISE reports, NREL data): 18% is realistic for modern PV modules. Previously standard for polycrystalline/monocrystalline panels. Now on lower end - modern panels typically achieve 20-25% efficiency.

privileged_info_summary: The task uses the real 4StoreyBuilding.inp model (3,258 lines, 94,825 bytes) from official DOE-2 training examples at doe2.com. The target PV system parameters are all verified as realistic and based on industry standards: 25 kW capacity is practical for commercial buildings, 20-degree tilt is widely used in utility-scale installations with <5% efficiency loss, 180-degree azimuth is true south (optimal for Northern Hemisphere), and 18% efficiency is realistic for modern photovoltaic modules (though now on the lower end of contemporary standards). The building model is not synthetic data.
